overview: A compression fitting creates a watertight seal by compressing a soft metal ring (the olive or ferrule) against a pipe when the nut is tightened. No soldering required — ideal for retrofits and repairs where heat cannot be applied. Available in straight, elbow, and tee configurations.
installationNotes: Ensure pipe end is cut square and deburred. Slide nut then olive onto pipe before inserting into fitting body. Tighten nut 1.5 turns past hand-tight — do not overtighten.

overview: A push-fit fitting connects pipes with no tools — simply push the pipe end in and internal collet teeth grip it securely. Widely used for quick repairs and for connecting copper, plastic, and CPVC pipes. Most brands are demountable with a release collar.
installationNotes: Ensure pipe is cut square; deburr and clean. Push firmly until pipe bottoms out — you'll feel and hear a click. Tug to confirm grip. Use release clip to demount.

overview: A gate valve uses a rising or non-rising metal wedge (gate) that moves perpendicular to flow to open or close. When fully open, the bore is completely unobstructed — hence 'full-bore valve'. Not designed for throttling; should be either fully open or fully closed.
installationNotes: Install with arrow indicating direction of flow (if marked). Gate valves can be installed in any orientation. Turn clockwise to close (conventional). Do not use for throttling — causes premature seat wear.

overview: A ball valve uses a rotating ball with a through-hole bore to control flow. A quarter-turn (90°) of the handle moves from fully open to fully closed. The lever handle position visually shows the valve state — handle in line with pipe = open, perpendicular = closed.
installationNotes: Install in either direction — ball valves are bidirectional. Lever must have clearance to rotate 90°. Leave valve in open position if used infrequently to prevent the PTFE seat from taking a set.

overview: A stop valve (or service valve) is used to isolate a single appliance — a tap, toilet cistern, or dishwasher — without shutting off the whole system. Operated by a flat-head screwdriver slot on the spindle. Slot in line with pipe = open; slot across pipe = closed.
installationNotes: Turn clockwise to close. Slot indicator: parallel to pipe = open, perpendicular = closed. Typically installed under basins, WCs, and behind appliances. Use correct size for pipe (most common 15mm).

overview: A P-trap is a curved section of waste pipe that retains a water seal after each use, preventing sewer gases and odours from entering the building. The water seal must be at least 50mm deep (per UK building regulations). Used under sinks, basins, and showers.
installationNotes: Install with trap outlet angled slightly downward for drainage. Do not use excessive slope — the water seal can siphon out. Ensure a minimum 50mm water seal depth. Clean-out access should be reachable.

overview: A float valve is used in cisterns (toilet tanks, cold water storage tanks) to maintain a set water level. As the cistern fills, the float rises with the water surface; when it reaches the set level, the float arm closes the valve. Modern versions use a float cup rather than a large ball float.
installationNotes: Adjust water level by changing float arm angle or adjusting the float height on modern cup-type valves. Ensure the bottom-entry or side-entry style matches the cistern. Use a service valve on the supply line for easy maintenance.

overview: A flexible stainless-braided hose used to connect a tap or appliance to the water supply. The flexible nature accommodates offset tap tails and tight under-sink spaces. Available in various lengths (typically 300–500mm) with standard fittings at each end.
materials: EPDM inner, stainless steel braid outer, brass fittings
installationNotes: Do not over-bend — minimum bend radius applies. Hand-tighten first, then one additional quarter-turn with spanner. Do not use PTFE tape on compression connections. Replace every 5–10 years as a precaution.

overview: A check valve allows flow in one direction only, automatically preventing backflow when flow reverses or pressure drops. The valve contains a spring-loaded or gravity-fed disc or ball that opens under forward pressure and closes under reverse pressure.
installationNotes: Install with flow direction arrow aligned with intended flow. Most check valves must be horizontal or have the cap pointing upward — check manufacturer guidance. Test function periodically on critical installations (e.g., hot water systems).

overview: A copper elbow fitting changes pipe direction by 90° (or 45° in offset elbows). Soldered (capillary) elbows are the most common form in copper installations. The 'street elbow' variant has one male and one female end, eliminating the need for a coupling on one side.
installationNotes: Clean both pipe and fitting bore with wire wool or abrasive cloth. Apply flux to both mating surfaces. Use lead-free solder for potable water systems. Heat fitting evenly; apply solder to joint — not the flame.

overview: A Schrader valve is the access port used by HVAC technicians to connect gauge manifold sets, charge refrigerant, and take system pressure readings. It contains a spring-loaded pin (the Schrader core) that opens when depressed by a manifold hose fitting. The same design is used in car tire valves.
installationNotes: Always keep valve caps installed when not in use to prevent contamination and refrigerant loss. Use a Schrader core removal tool to replace worn cores without evacuating the system. Torque to manufacturer specification when installing new cores.

overview: The TXV regulates the flow of refrigerant entering the evaporator coil by sensing suction line temperature (via a remote bulb) and evaporator pressure. It maintains a constant superheat at the evaporator outlet, maximizing efficiency. The TXV is the most common metering device in split-system air conditioning and heat pumps.
installationNotes: Strap the remote sensing bulb tightly to the suction line at the 4 or 8 o'clock position (not bottom — to avoid oil pooling affecting temperature reading). Insulate the bulb from ambient air. Adjust superheat only after system has reached steady state.

overview: A filter drier removes moisture, acid, and particulate contamination from the refrigerant circuit. Installed in the liquid line, it contains a desiccant (silica gel or molecular sieve) that captures moisture before it can reach the TXV or other metering devices where it could freeze and block flow.
installationNotes: Replace after any system open-to-atmosphere event (repair, component replacement). Install with flow direction arrow correct. Always install a new drier when replacing the compressor. Bi-flow driers are used on heat pump reversing systems.

overview: A volume control damper is a flat or multi-blade device installed inside ductwork to regulate and balance airflow through a zone or branch. Manual VCDs are adjusted with a handle or screwdriver slot and locked in position; motorized versions are controlled by a building management system or zone controller.
installationNotes: Mark position clearly after balancing (open/closed %). Motorized versions require 24V or 230V actuator. Install with handle accessible for future adjustment. Do not confuse with fire dampers — fire dampers are a life-safety device and require fusible link or actuator reset.

overview: The condenser fan motor drives the propeller fan on the outdoor unit of a split system, drawing ambient air across the condenser coil to reject heat from the refrigerant. Failure causes high head pressure and compressor cutout. Most models are PSC (permanent split capacitor) motors requiring an external run capacitor.
installationNotes: Match RPM, horsepower, and rotation direction (typically counterclockwise when viewed from shaft end on outdoor units). Always replace the run capacitor when replacing the motor. Ensure blade pitch is correct after installation.

overview: The evaporator coil is the indoor heat exchanger in a split air conditioning or heat pump system. Low-pressure liquid refrigerant absorbs heat from indoor air as it evaporates, cooling the air. The 'A-coil' shape is the most common configuration, with two sets of fins meeting at a peak to maximize surface area.
installationNotes: Ensure proper refrigerant charge — undercharge causes icing, overcharge causes liquid slugging. Clean coil annually to maintain heat transfer. Drain pan and drain line must be clear to prevent water overflow. Match coil capacity to outdoor unit.

overview: An expansion vessel accommodates the increase in water volume when the heating system heats up, preventing the pressure relief valve from opening on every heat cycle. It contains a pre-pressurised nitrogen cushion separated from the system water by a rubber diaphragm. The pre-charge pressure must match the system static pressure.
installationNotes: Check and set pre-charge pressure (nitrogen side) equal to static system height in bar (1 bar per 10m height) when system is cold and depressurised. Install on return pipe, ideally close to circulator pump suction. Vessel must be accessible for pressure checks.

overview: A circulator pump moves hot water around the heating system from the boiler to radiators and back. Modern circulators are 'wet rotor' design — the motor rotor runs in the water being pumped, eliminating shaft seals and gland leaks. Most modern models are variable-speed (ECM motor) for energy efficiency.
installationNotes: Install with shaft horizontal for correct bearing lubrication. Arrow on pump body indicates flow direction — install on the return pipe (cold side), pump facing into the boiler. Bleed air from pump via bleed screw on startup. Match pump head to system resistance.

overview: A pressure relief valve is a safety device that opens automatically if system pressure exceeds its set point (typically 3 bar on domestic heating systems), preventing boiler or vessel rupture. On unvented hot water cylinders, a temperature and pressure (T&P) valve also opens if water temperature exceeds 90–95°C.
installationNotes: Discharge pipe must run to a safe location where hot water will not cause injury or property damage — typically outside, low to the ground, with an air break. Never cap or bypass a PRV. Test annually by lifting the lever briefly. Replace if it fails to reseat or continues to drip.

overview: A zone valve is an electrically actuated valve that opens or closes to direct hot water to a specific heating zone (e.g., upstairs or downstairs circuit, or the hot water cylinder). Two-port zone valves are either open or closed; three-port mid-position valves can direct flow to heating only, hot water only, or both simultaneously.
installationNotes: Ensure correct orientation per manufacturer arrow. The microswitch at end-of-travel supplies live to the boiler and pump. Wire to S-plan (2-port) or Y-plan (3-port mid-position) wiring diagram. Allow 2–3 minutes for spring-return on power loss.

overview: A filling loop is a short hose or rigid connection used to temporarily connect the cold mains supply to the sealed central heating system to fill or re-pressurise it. It is removed or closed after use to maintain a physical break between the potable water supply and the heating system (a regulations requirement in the UK).
installationNotes: Open both valves slowly and monitor pressure gauge — target 1–1.5 bar cold fill pressure. Close both valves once filled; disconnect external hose filling loops after each use (Water Regulations requirement). Do not leave permanently connected — this would create a cross-connection to the domestic water supply.

overview: A TRV controls room temperature by regulating hot water flow into a radiator based on the ambient air temperature sensed by its wax capsule head. As room temperature rises, the wax expands, progressively closing the valve to reduce heat output. The numbered dial (1–5) sets the target temperature.
materials: wax element in plastic or chrome head
installationNotes: Install on the flow side of the radiator (supply pipe into radiator). The TRV head must have unrestricted airflow around it — do not cover with curtains or fit behind furniture. Pair with a lockshield valve on the return to enable balancing. One room in the house should have a room stat instead of a TRV to act as the reference zone.

overview: A lockshield valve is fitted on the return side of a radiator, opposite the TRV or hand-wheel valve. Its flow rate is set during commissioning to 'balance' the heating system — ensuring each radiator gets the correct water flow. The protective cap prevents casual adjustment, preserving the balance setting.
installationNotes: Set using a lockshield key or adjustable spanner during commissioning. The number of turns from fully closed varies per radiator based on its distance from the pump. Record turns open for each radiator for future reference. Radiators closest to the pump get more turns closed.

overview: A plate heat exchanger (PHE) transfers heat between two fluid circuits through a stack of corrugated metal plates, without the fluids mixing. In boilers, it separates the primary heating water from the domestic hot water circuit. Brazed plate types use copper or nickel brazing, making them compact and highly efficient.
installationNotes: Ensure correct primary/secondary flow connections (cross-flow configuration for maximum efficiency). Flush before installation to remove debris. Scale inhibitor in the primary circuit extends PHE life. De-scale with approved chemicals if flow rate drops — do not use mechanical cleaning on brazed plates.

overview: Standard 15mm copper pipe used for domestic water supply and heating systems. Durable, corrosion-resistant, and supports both solder and compression fittings.
installationNotes: Ensure pipe is clean and square. Use 15mm pipe cutters. Deburr before fitting.

overview: Flexible plastic pipe with an internal oxygen barrier layer to prevent corrosion in heating systems. Compatible with push-fit and press-fit fittings. Easier to route through joists than rigid copper.
installationNotes: Always use pipe inserts (liners) when using push-fit fittings. Do not use with compression fittings unless specified by manufacturer.

overview: A simple fitting used to join two lengths of 15mm pipe in a straight line. Available in solder, compression, and push-fit versions.
installationNotes: Ensure both pipe ends are fully inserted into the center stop of the fitting.

overview: A wall-mounted tap typically installed outdoors for garden hose connection. Features a threaded outlet for hose connectors.
installationNotes: Install with a check valve (double check valve) inside the property to prevent backflow contamination of the potable supply.

overview: A small valve at the top of a radiator used to release trapped air, ensuring the entire radiator fills with hot water and heats efficiently.
installationNotes: Use a bleed key. Only vent when the system is off or the pump is not running to avoid drawing in more air.

overview: A thin, white tape used to seal threaded pipe joints. It acts as a lubricant and filler, ensuring a watertight seal on tapered threads.
installationNotes: Apply 5-10 wraps in the direction of the thread (clockwise as you look at the end of the pipe).

overview: Solder alloy used to create permanent capillary joints in copper pipework. Required by law for potable water systems.
installationNotes: Use with flux. Heat the fitting, not the solder wire directly.

overview: The primary valve that controls the entry of the water supply into a property. Usually located under the kitchen sink or near the property boundary.
installationNotes: Exercise the valve twice a year to prevent it from seizing up. Ensure it is accessible for emergencies.

overview: A pump designed to increase water pressure in systems with low flow. Often used for high-end showers or in buildings where mains pressure is insufficient.
installationNotes: Requires an electrical supply. Ensure a steady water supply is available at the suction side to prevent cavitation.

overview: An all-in-one unit that provides both central heating and instantaneous domestic hot water without the need for a separate tank.
installationNotes: Must be installed by a Gas Safe registered engineer (UK) or certified professional. Requires flue and drainage for condensate.

overview: Flexible 22mm plastic pipe for water supply and heating systems.
installationNotes: Use pipe inserts for all push-fit connections.

overview: A T-shaped fitting for joining three 15mm pipes.
installationNotes: Ensure all branches are fully inserted.

overview: A small inline valve for isolating sections of pipework.
installationNotes: Install with flow arrow in correct direction.

overview: Standard 40mm plastic waste pipe for sinks and appliances.
installationNotes: Use solvent weld or compression fittings.

overview: Central unit for circulating and conditioning air in HVAC systems.
installationNotes: Ensure filters are accessible for maintenance.

overview: A 90° bend for changing direction in ductwork.
installationNotes: Seal all joints to prevent air leaks.

overview: A fitting for connecting ducts of different diameters.
installationNotes: Install with larger end upstream for airflow.

overview: A chamber for distributing air to multiple ducts.
installationNotes: Ensure plenum is insulated to prevent condensation.

overview: Electrical capacitor for starting and running HVAC motors.
installationNotes: Discharge before handling. Replace if bulged.

overview: A window for checking refrigerant condition and moisture in the system.
installationNotes: Check for bubbles to diagnose low charge.

overview: Electronic controller for managing multiple HVAC zones.
installationNotes: Wire according to manufacturer's diagram.

overview: Instrument for measuring system pressure in heating circuits.
installationNotes: Install at eye level for easy reading.

overview: Device for automatically releasing trapped air from heating systems.
installationNotes: Install at high points in the system.

overview: Thermostat to protect heating systems from freezing.
installationNotes: Set to activate above freezing point.

overview: Filter for removing magnetic debris from heating water.
installationNotes: Clean annually to maintain efficiency.

overview: Pump for removing condensate from condensing boilers.
installationNotes: Install with check valve to prevent backflow.

useCases: Joining copper pipes in domestic plumbing
Repairs where soldering is not possible (e.g., near timber or insulation)
installationNotes: Do not overtighten — typically 1 to 1.25 turns past hand-tight. Use PTFE tape or jointing compound on the olive for extra security. Always use pipe inserts when connecting to plastic pipe.
overview: A mechanical fitting that joins two pipes or a pipe to a fixture using a compression nut, a brass olive (ring), and a fitting body. Tightening the nut compresses the olive against the pipe, creating a watertight seal without soldering.

useCases: Fast repairs and joins without heat or tools
Tight spaces where a wrench can't reach
installationNotes: Ensure pipe is cut square and deburred. Push pipe in until it hits the internal stop — mark the insertion depth on the pipe first. Pipe inserts are required for plastic pipes. To disconnect, push the release collar toward the fitting and pull the pipe out.
overview: A fitting that connects pipes by simply pushing the pipe into the fitting body. Internal grab ring teeth grip the pipe, while an O-ring creates the seal. No tools, solder, or compression nuts required. Can be removed and reused.

useCases: Main water shut-off for a building
Isolating sections of a plumbing system
installationNotes: Gate valves should only be used fully open or fully closed. Using one partially open will cause the gate to erode and the valve to fail. Replace with a ball valve if throttling is needed. Operate the handwheel periodically to prevent seizing.
overview: A valve that controls flow by raising or lowering an internal gate (wedge) via a handwheel. When fully open, provides unrestricted full-bore flow. Designed for fully open or fully closed positions — not for throttling or regulating flow.

useCases: Isolating water supply to fixtures or appliances
Gas line shut-off (yellow handle, gas-rated models only)
installationNotes: Red or blue handles indicate hot/cold water. Yellow handles indicate gas-rated valves — never use a water-rated ball valve on gas. Lever should be parallel to pipe when open, perpendicular when closed.
overview: A valve with a hollow, perforated ball inside that pivots 90° to open or close flow. The lever handle makes operation fast — a quarter turn from fully open to fully closed. Offers reliable, tight shut-off and is widely used as the modern replacement for gate valves.

useCases: Isolating individual fixtures (sinks, toilets, washing machines)
Main stopcock for a domestic property
installationNotes: The washer inside will wear over time and can be replaced without removing the valve body. Arrow on the body indicates flow direction — install accordingly. The main stopcock is typically located where the mains supply enters the building.
overview: A valve that stops or starts flow using a washer pressed against a seat by a screw-down mechanism. The handwheel is turned multiple times to fully open or close. Often found under sinks and behind toilets for local fixture isolation.

connectionType: Compression waste / Solvent weld / Push-fit waste
compatibility: Basin, sink, bath, and shower outlets
useCases: Under every sink, basin, bath, and shower
Connecting fixtures to the waste drainage system
Preventing sewer gas entry into occupied spaces
installationNotes: Ensure the trap is properly filled with water after installation — run the tap. If a fixture is unused for long periods, the water seal can evaporate (pour water in periodically). The trap depth (water seal) should be at least 50mm. Clean regularly to prevent blockages.
overview: A curved section of pipe beneath a drain that retains a small amount of water, creating a seal that prevents sewer gases from entering the building. The 'P' shape refers to the profile when viewed from the side. Every plumbed fixture must have a trap.

useCases: Filling toilet cisterns to correct level
Maintaining water level in loft storage tanks
Feed and expansion tank for heating systems
installationNotes: Adjust the float arm or screw to set the correct water level — water should be about 25mm below the overflow. Part 1 valves are for high-pressure mains; Part 2 valves are for low-pressure tank-fed systems. Check the washer if the valve drips or doesn't shut off fully.
overview: A valve that automatically controls water level in a tank or cistern. A buoyant float rises with the water level and, through a lever arm or piston mechanism, shuts off the inlet when the desired level is reached. Found in toilet cisterns, cold water storage tanks, and header tanks.

useCases: Preventing backflow from a heating system into mains water
At the outlet of booster pumps
Where water regulations require backflow protection (e.g., filling loops)
installationNotes: Install with the arrow on the body pointing in the direction of desired flow. Double-check valves are required where the risk category is higher. Must be testable and accessible for inspection in commercial installations.
overview: A valve that allows fluid to flow in one direction only. An internal flap, disc, or spring mechanism opens with forward flow and closes automatically if flow reverses, preventing backflow contamination. Required by water regulations at many connection points.

useCases: Supporting horizontal pipe runs to prevent sagging
Fixing pipes in place before plastering or boxing in
installationNotes: Space clips at recommended intervals: 1.2m for horizontal 15mm copper, 1.8m for vertical. Use the correct size — a loose clip won't support the pipe. Allow for thermal expansion on long runs of plastic pipe (use slide clips, not fixed). Use plastic clips on plastic pipe to avoid damage.
overview: A bracket or clip used to secure pipes to walls, floors, joists, or other structural surfaces. Keeps pipework neat, supported, and prevents movement or stress on joints. Available in plastic snap-in, metal saddle, and heavy-duty split ring styles.

useCases: Isolating a single tap for washer replacement
Shutting off water to a toilet for maintenance
Installed on each supply line to every fixture in a modern installation
installationNotes: Slot parallel to pipe = open. Slot perpendicular to pipe = closed. Building regulations now require isolation valves on the supply to every fixture. Compact size allows installation in tight spaces under basins and behind toilets.
overview: A small inline valve used to isolate the water supply to an individual fixture or appliance without shutting off the entire system. Operated by turning the slotted head 90° with a screwdriver or coin — no handwheel required.

useCases: Changing pipe direction around corners and obstacles
Routing pipe runs through walls and floors
installationNotes: Clean pipe and fitting with wire wool before soldering. Apply flux evenly. For solder-ring fittings, heat evenly until a ring of solder appears around the joint. Let cool without disturbing. Compression elbows are used where heat is not safe (near timber, insulation).
overview: A fitting that changes the direction of a pipe run by 90° (or 45° in angled variants). Available as end-feed (requires solder), solder-ring (pre-loaded solder), or compression types. One of the most commonly used fittings in domestic plumbing.

useCases: Branching a hot or cold supply to a new fixture
installationNotes: When soldering, feed all three joints at once — heat the fitting body, not the pipe. On a live system, use a compression or push-fit tee to avoid draining down. Ensure flow direction is correct on reducing tees.
overview: A T-shaped fitting that creates a branch connection from a main pipe run. Used to split or combine flow. Available as equal tees (all ports same size) or reducing tees (branch is a smaller diameter). Available in solder, compression, and push-fit types.

useCases: Transitioning from a 22mm main run to 15mm branch supply
Connecting older large-diameter pipework to modern smaller sizes
Reducing pipe size near terminal fixtures
installationNotes: Always reduce in the direction of flow where possible. Use eccentric reducers on horizontal runs to keep the bottom of the pipe flush and avoid air locks. Available in solder-ring and end-feed types.
overview: A fitting that connects two pipes of different diameters, allowing a transition between pipe sizes. Concentric reducers keep the centerline aligned; eccentric reducers offset the centerline (used to prevent air pockets in horizontal runs).

installationNotes: Mount the sensing bulb at 10 or 2 o'clock on the suction line (never at the bottom — oil collects there). Insulate the bulb to prevent false readings from ambient air. Superheat adjustment screw: clockwise = more superheat (less refrigerant), counterclockwise = less superheat.
overview: A precision metering device that regulates the flow of liquid refrigerant into the evaporator based on the superheat at the evaporator outlet. A sensing bulb attached to the suction line detects temperature changes and adjusts the valve opening to maintain optimal superheat.

compatibility: Matched outdoor condensing unit (same brand/AHRI rating)
useCases: Paired with heat pump or AC condensing unit
installationNotes: Must be matched to the outdoor unit by AHRI certification for rated efficiency. Condensate drain must be properly trapped and routed. Filter should be accessible for regular replacement. Install on a drain pan in attic locations to catch condensate overflow.
overview: The indoor component of a split HVAC system containing the blower fan, evaporator coil, air filter, and sometimes supplemental electric heat strips. Draws return air from the building, passes it over the coil (for cooling or heating), and distributes conditioned air through the ductwork.

useCases: Fire safety in commercial ductwork penetrations
installationNotes: Manual dampers: lever parallel to duct = open, perpendicular = closed. Fire dampers must be accessible for inspection — install access doors. Motorized dampers need 24V power and control signal from thermostat or BAS. Label all dampers clearly for future technicians.
overview: A device installed inside ductwork that regulates or blocks airflow. Manual dampers use a lever to adjust a blade; motorized dampers are controlled by thermostats or building automation systems. Fire dampers are safety devices that close automatically when heat-activated.

sizes: Replacement must match boiler model exactly
compatibility: Specific to boiler make and model
useCases: Core component of every boiler — transfers combustion heat to water
Secondary DHW heating in combi boilers
External plate heat exchangers for system separation
installationNotes: Scaling and sludge are the main enemies — a clean system with inhibitor protects the heat exchanger. Kettling noise (rumbling/banging) usually indicates scale buildup on the heat exchanger. Flushing the system with a powerflush machine can restore performance. Replacement is model-specific and often requires significant boiler disassembly.
overview: A device that transfers heat between two fluids without mixing them. In boilers, the primary heat exchanger transfers heat from the gas burner flame to the heating water. In combi boilers, a secondary plate heat exchanger heats domestic hot water from the primary heating water.

compatibility: Standard M30 × 1.5 head connection (most European brands)
useCases: Energy saving — reduces flow to radiators in warm rooms
Required by building regulations on most radiators in new installations
installationNotes: Do NOT fit a TRV in the same room as the room thermostat — they will conflict. Fit on the flow side of the radiator (lockshield on the return). Keep the TRV head away from heat sources and curtains that could give false readings. Smart TRV heads are available that replace the mechanical head with app-controlled versions.
overview: A self-regulating valve fitted to a radiator that controls room temperature by adjusting water flow based on the surrounding air temperature. A wax or liquid-filled sensor in the valve head expands and contracts to open or close the valve pin — no electrical power needed.

useCases: Temporary or permanent termination of a pipe run
installationNotes: Clean both the pipe end and inside of the cap with PVC primer before applying PVC cement. Apply cement to both surfaces and push together with a quarter turn. Hold for 10 seconds. Allow full cure time before pressurizing.
overview: A Charlotte Pipe PVC Schedule 40 pressure cap that fits over the end of a 1/2" PVC pipe to seal it. Socket (slip) connection — glued onto the pipe end using PVC cement. Used to terminate unused pipe runs or for pressure testing.

useCases: Branching a supply line to a threaded valve or fixture
Creating distribution points in irrigation systems
Connecting threaded accessories to a PVC pipe run
installationNotes: Solvent weld the socket ends. Use PTFE tape on any threaded connections. Do not overtighten threaded joints in PVC — plastic threads are more fragile than metal.
overview: A Charlotte Pipe PVC Schedule 40 tee fitting with two socket (slip) ends and one female pipe thread (FPT) branch. Allows branching a pipe run and connecting to a threaded component on the branch outlet.

useCases: Temporary or permanent termination of a pipe run
installationNotes: Use PVC primer and cement. Push together with a quarter turn and hold for 10 seconds.
overview: A Charlotte Pipe PVC Schedule 40 pressure cap that fits over the end of a 3/4" PVC pipe to seal it. Socket (slip) connection — glued onto the pipe end using PVC cement.

installationNotes: Apply PTFE tape to the male threads before screwing in. Hand-tighten plus one turn with a wrench — do not overtighten PVC threads.
overview: A Charlotte Pipe PVC Schedule 40 plug with male pipe thread (MPT). Screws into a female-threaded fitting to seal the opening. Used to cap off threaded outlets in PVC piping systems.

useCases: Reducing pipe size within an existing fitting
Transitioning from larger to smaller supply lines
Adapting fittings when exact size matches aren't available
installationNotes: Apply PVC primer and cement to both the bushing exterior and the receiving fitting socket. Insert fully and hold.
overview: A Charlotte Pipe PVC Schedule 40 reducer bushing that steps down from 3/4" to 1/2". The spigot end fits into a 3/4" fitting socket, and the internal socket accepts a 1/2" pipe. Allows transition between pipe sizes within a fitting.

useCases: Connecting PVC pipe to a threaded valve
Transitioning from PVC to metal piping
installationNotes: Solvent weld the socket end to PVC pipe. Apply PTFE tape to the male threads before connecting to threaded fittings. Do not over-tighten.
overview: A PVC Schedule 40 male adapter with a socket (slip) end and a male pipe thread (MPT) end. Allows transitioning from a solvent-weld PVC system to a threaded connection for valves, metal fittings, or equipment.

compatibility: 1/2" copper pipe (Type K, L, or M)
useCases: Capping off copper pipe ends in hydronic heating systems
Quick repairs without soldering near combustible materials
installationNotes: Mark the insertion depth on the pipe. Clean and deburr the pipe end. Insert pipe fully into the fitting and press with the appropriate jaw size. A green dot on Viega fittings indicates it has NOT been pressed — the dot disappears after pressing.
overview: A Viega ProPress 1/2" copper cap (CA-01) for sealing the end of a copper pipe run using press-fit technology. No soldering required — crimped onto the pipe with a pressing tool for a permanent, watertight seal.

compatibility: 1/2" copper pipe (Type K, L, or M)
useCases: Joining copper pipe sections in boiler piping
Repairs in confined spaces where soldering isn't safe
installationNotes: Deburr and clean pipe ends. Mark insertion depth. Press both ends with the correct jaw size. Verify the green dot is gone on both sides after pressing.
overview: A Viega ProPress 1/2" copper coupling that joins two pieces of copper pipe using press-fit technology. No solder, flux, or open flame required. Ideal for boiler and heating system installations where speed and fire safety matter.

compatibility: 3/4" FPT fittings, valves, and equipment (threaded end)
useCases: Connecting ProPress copper piping to a boiler
Transitioning to threaded zone valves or circulators
installationNotes: Press the copper end first, then thread into the equipment. Apply PTFE tape or thread sealant to the male threads. Do not use a pipe wrench on the copper press portion.
overview: A Viega ProPress 3/4" adapter (AD-02) that transitions from a press-fit copper connection to a male NPT threaded connection. Allows connecting ProPress copper piping to threaded valves, boilers, and equipment without soldering.

compatibility: 1/2" copper pipe (Type K, L, or M)
useCases: Gradual direction changes in copper piping
Routing around obstacles with less flow restriction than a 90°
Water supply and drainage vent piping
installationNotes: Clean pipe and fitting interior with emery cloth. Apply flux, assemble, and solder. The gentler angle creates less friction loss than a 90° elbow.
overview: An Everbilt 1/2" copper 45° elbow with CxC (cup × cup / sweat × sweat) connections. Changes pipe direction by 45 degrees with a gentler turn than a 90° elbow, reducing flow restriction. Joined by soldering.

useCases: Tight spaces where a standard elbow + pipe stub won't fit
Connecting directly into another fitting (e.g., tee, valve)
Reducing the number of solder joints in a run

overview: An 18-inch braided stainless steel flexible connector with push-to-connect ends on both sides. Used for pipe repairs where a rigid coupling cannot be easily installed, or where flexibility is needed to connect two fixed pipe ends that are slightly misaligned. Available in ½" and ¾".

applications: Water softener supply and bypass connections
Water treatment equipment hook-ups
Any connection requiring a 24" flexible line with push inlet and FIP outlet
installationNotes: Thread FIP end onto 1" male threaded port on the water softener with PTFE tape. Push ¾" supply pipe into push end. The 24" length is specifically designed for standard water softener cabinet spacings.
dimensionKey: hoseLength = overall flexible hose length in inches
source: NIBCO Push Fittings Valves & Flexible Connectors PTC-0818-R041624
overview: A 24-inch braided stainless steel flexible connector with a push-to-connect end and a female iron pipe (FIP) threaded end. Specifically sized for water softener bypass and inlet connections where the longer 24" length accommodates the wider spacing of water softener cabinet connections. Available in ¾" × 1".

applications: Releasing push-to-connect fittings for repositioning
Removing push-fit fittings during remodels
Correcting misaligned push-fit connections
installationNotes: Slide the tool over the pipe up to the fitting collar. Push the tool in firmly against the fitting face to depress the release collar, then pull the pipe straight out. Keep one tool per pipe size on every job with push fittings.
source: NIBCO Push Fittings Valves & Flexible Connectors PTC-0818-R041624
overview: A small U-shaped plastic disconnect tool used to release push-to-connect fittings without damaging the fitting or pipe. Insert the tool around the pipe at the fitting inlet to depress the release collar, then pull the pipe out. The only correct way to remove a push-fit connection — attempting to pull without the tool can damage the grip ring and cause leaks.
